scenario: A team's polyglot architecture has Java services + Node.js services + Go microservices. They need to integrate all with Camunda 8.
question: Comparison of Camunda 8 SDKs across languages -- Java, Node.js, Go?

options.text: 7+) at apis-tools/camunda-spring-boot-starter/ -- @JobWorker annotation, @Autowired, @Deployment. md; modern async/await API; cross-component (Zeebe + Operate + Tasklist). ). Async and sync clients supported. 8. 8. proto files via community-clients/.

Java has the most mature offering via the Camunda Spring Boot Starter. js are supported through the @camunda8/sdk package with full type definitions and a modern async/await API. 8, with dedicated documentation covering quick-start, job workers, and resource deployment. C# also has a first-class SDK. Go is supported via community clients and gRPC code generation from Camunda's proto files.

Claiming that all Camunda 8 SDK languages are equally featured is only partially correct. While all first-class SDKs support the core Camunda 8 operations (job activation and completion, message publishing, decision evaluation, process instance creation), maturity and depth of features vary. The Java Camunda Spring Boot Starter is the most mature, offering deep Spring Boot integration, auto-configuration, and annotation-driven development. js SDK provides cross-component coverage. 8 but may have a narrower feature set compared to Java. Go relies on community clients rather than an officially maintained SDK page.

Claiming that the Camunda 8 SDKs are functionally identical across all supported languages overstates the uniformity. While all SDKs expose the same underlying Camunda 8 capabilities, the idiomatic style and feature depth differ significantly. The Java SDK uses Spring Boot annotations and reflection-driven binding. The TypeScript/Node SDK uses async/await with explicit promise handling. Python uses both async and sync client patterns. The APIs wrap the same gRPC and REST protocols, but the developer experience, community support, ecosystem maturity, and auxiliary features like auto-retry and back-pressure handling are not uniform across all language clients.

a.text: Correct. Camunda 8's first-class SDKs: Java (most mature) -- apis-tools/camunda-spring-boot-starter/: Camunda Spring Boot Starter (renamed from "Spring Zeebe" in 8.7+): full Spring Boot integration; auto-configuration; @JobWorker for handlers; @Variable for parameter binding. CamundaClient (plain Java): lower-level; useful for non-Spring apps. Unified SDK across Zeebe + Operate + Tasklist APIs via the Orchestration Cluster API. TypeScript / Node.js (@camunda8/sdk) -- apis-tools/typescript/camunda8-sdk.md: TypeScript-first: full type definitions. Modern API: async/await; handler returns auto-complete jobs; explicit job.complete() / job.error() / job.fail() available. Cross-component: Zeebe + Operate + Tasklist APIs. Python -- first-class since 8.8 at apis-tools/python-sdk/: Quick-start zero-config + programmatic configuration paths. Async and sync clients. Job workers, deploy resources, create process instance, semantic types. C# -- first-class at apis-tools/csharp-sdk/. Go: Not currently a Camunda-maintained apis-tools/go-sdk/ page in 8.8. Community Go clients listed under community-clients/. Pure gRPC client gen from proto files is supported. Other languages: REST API (Orchestration Cluster API): any HTTP client. gRPC generated: codegen from Camunda's proto files. Choose by: Team expertise: pick the language your team knows. Existing stack: align with your services' tech. Performance needs: gRPC (Java / Node / Go SDKs) faster than REST; high-throughput workers prefer SDKs.
